From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Belgian award for young artists

This article is about the Belgian government prize. For other uses, see [Prix de Rome (disambiguation)](/wiki/Prix_de_Rome_(disambiguation) "Prix de Rome (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Medailleverso.jpg/250px-Medailleverso.jpg)](/wiki/File:Medailleverso.jpg)

Prize medal received by [Constant Montald](/wiki/Constant_Montald "Constant Montald") in 1886

The Belgian **Prix de Rome** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Prijs van Rome*) is an award for young artists, created in 1832, following the example of the original French [Prix de Rome](/wiki/Prix_de_Rome "Prix de Rome"). The [Royal Academy of Fine Arts Antwerp](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Fine_Arts_Antwerp "Royal Academy of Fine Arts Antwerp") organised the prize until 1920, when the national government took over. The first prize is also sometimes called the **Grand Prix de Rome**. There were distinct categories for painting, sculpture, architecture and music. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Prix_de_Rome_(Belgium)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The Prix de Rome was a [scholarship](/wiki/Scholarship "Scholarship") for arts students. It was created in 1663 in [France](/wiki/France "France") under the reign of [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"). It was an annual burse for promising artists (painters, sculptors, and architects) who proved their talents by completing a very difficult elimination contest. The prize, organised by the [Royal Academy of Painting and Sculpture](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Painting_and_Sculpture "Royal Academy of Painting and Sculpture") (*Académie royale de peinture et de sculpture*), was open to their students. The award winner would win a stay at the [Palazzo Mancini](/wiki/Palazzo_Mancini "Palazzo Mancini") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") at the expense of the King of France. The stay could be extended if the director of the institution deemed it desirable.

Expanded after 140 years into five categories, the contest started in 1663 as three categories – [painting](/wiki/Painting "Painting"), [sculpting](/wiki/Sculpting "Sculpting"), and [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture"); in 1803, [music](/wiki/Music "Music") was added; in 1804, [engraving](/wiki/Engraving "Engraving") was added. The winner of the "First Grand Prize" (called the *agréé*)[[1]](#cite_note-1) would be sent to [The Academy of France in Rome](/wiki/French_Academy_in_Rome "French Academy in Rome") founded by [Jean-Baptiste Colbert](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Colbert "Jean-Baptiste Colbert") in 1666. In 1807, [Louis Napoleon](/wiki/Louis_Napoleon "Louis Napoleon") created the [Dutch version](/wiki/Prix_de_Rome_(Netherlands) "Prix de Rome (Netherlands)") of the Prix de Rome. After the creation of Belgium as an independent state in 1830, the Belgian government started their own version of the Prix de Rome in 1832. [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
